hcd_data.architectural_style: House-form, Semi-detached, Bay-and-Gable (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: The property has value as an early-twentieth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, the property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. The property retains most of its original building features including its cross-gable roofline, double-height bay, brick cladding with decorative brickwork, and fenestration pattern with flat-headed openings. Contextually, the property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. The property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

facade_detail.composition: The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 3 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its semi-detached bay-and-gable house-form typology.
facade_detail.opening_rhythm: The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2, 1 window bays from lower to upper levels, using double-hung sash windows, and 1 principal entrance.
facade_detail.heritage_expression: Character-defining facade elements include decorative brick, original windows.
facade_detail.heritage_summary: The property retains most of its original building features including its cross-gable roofline, double-height bay, brick cladding with decorative brickwork, and fenestration pattern with flat-headed openings. Contextually, the property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street.

facade_description: 22 Lippincott St presents a bay-and-gable domestic facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 3 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its semi-detached bay-and-gable house-form typology. The facade reads primarily as brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2, 1 window bays from lower to upper levels, using double-hung sash windows, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include decorative brick, original windows. HCD note: The property has value as an early-twentieth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, the property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. The property retains most of its original building features including its cross-gable roofline, double-height bay, brick cladding with decorative brickwork, and fenestration pattern with flat-headed openings. Contextually, the property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. The property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.
